evals.prompt: Our PR test runs produce no output engineers can use. They see 'tests failed' but no detail. What's the minimum setup?
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should recommend the baseline: (1) emit JUnit XML from every test runner; (2) configure CI to surface JUnit XML in its native check-run / test-results view; (3) upload failure artifacts (screenshots / videos / traces / logs) as CI artifacts with descriptive names; (4) link build ID into artifact names so triage is one click. Should suggest one richer HTML reporter (Playwright HTML / Allure / mochawesome) for richer triage. Should warn not to over-invest in dashboards before this baseline works. Cross-references ci-test-orchestration.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Recommends emitting JUnit XML as baseline
Recommends CI native test view integration
Warns against over-engineering before baseline works

evals.prompt: We have 4 shards running Playwright in parallel. Each emits its own HTML report. How do we get a single report?
evals.expected_output: Should introduce Playwright's `merge-reports` command: each shard runs with `--reporter=blob` (or stores its raw results), an aggregator job downloads all shard artifacts and runs `npx playwright merge-reports --reporter html ./blob-*` to produce a single HTML report. Show the GHA workflow shape with a `needs: [test]` aggregator job using `actions/download-artifact`. Should mention `if: always()` so aggregation runs even when shards fail. Should NOT recommend asking engineers to read 4 separate reports.

evals.prompt: should we use allure for our pytest suite
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should give a balanced answer: Allure is a strong choice for cross-runner step-level HTML reporting with attachments. Pros: rich step + attachment model, supports `@allure.step` decorators, can show trends if you deploy Allure server, supports many runners. Cons: requires an adapter library (`allure-pytest`), generated as a two-step process (raw results → render HTML), and a deployed Allure server adds opex. For small teams, Playwright HTML / mochawesome / pytest-html are often simpler. Recommend Allure when you outgrow per-PR reports and want trend / history. Cross-references pytest and the relevant runner skill.
assertions: Notes the two-step rendering and server-deployment cost
Suggests simpler reporters for small teams

evals.prompt: We built a Grafana dashboard for test trends. Nobody looks at it. What went wrong?
evals.expected_output: Should explain a common reporting anti-pattern: built dashboard without an audience first. Recommend: (1) talk to engineers, QA, and managers to find out what decisions they actually want to make from data; (2) prioritize metrics that drive a decision (e.g., 'top 10 flakiest tests this week' is actionable; 'aggregate pass rate' is not); (3) make the dashboard reachable from places people already look (CI pull request comments, Slack alerts on regression); (4) decide ownership and review cadence — without it the dashboard rots. Should warn against building more dashboards as the fix. Cross-references flaky-test-management for actionable flake metrics.
assertions: Recommends interviewing audience for decision-driving metrics
Recommends embedding metrics where people already look
Refuses to build more dashboards as the fix

evals.prompt: Our CI artifact storage costs are growing — we're keeping screenshots and videos of every test forever.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose lack of retention policy. Recommend: (1) most CI providers default to 30-90 day retention — verify and configure; (2) only capture videos on E2E failures, not passing runs (most UI frameworks support this); (3) capture screenshots on failure only, not on every action; (4) audit artifact sizes — sometimes one runaway logger floods storage; (5) for must-keep artifacts (e.g., regulatory audit), move them to cheaper S3 / archival storage out of CI's hot tier. Should warn about deleting unique evidence for past production incidents — preserve those. Cross-references test-environment-management for cost discipline.
assertions: Suggests archival storage for must-keep evidence

evals.prompt: Should we adopt Datadog CI Visibility for test analytics?
evals.expected_output: Should give a balanced trade-off. Pros: language-agnostic, push test results, sessions, and metrics; gets flake detection, trend analytics, owner / file attribution; works across multiple CI providers. Cons: commercial / paid; another data destination to manage; adds startup latency to test runs (tracer); team has to actually look at it. Recommend a pilot — one repo for 60 days; measure whether the data drives different behavior. Alternative: Buildkite Test Analytics, Trunk, Launchable, hand-rolled Grafana + your CI API. Should defer to qa-context.md (existing observability stack, budget, team analytics maturity).
assertions: Names DD CI Visibility's concrete strengths
